summary: Jeff Atwood examines a Wichita State usability study comparing reading speeds across twelve common web fonts. The study found a 40-second difference between the worst font (Corsiva) and best performers for two pages of text. Surprisingly, Courier—the only monospace font tested—was the second-worst performer, which Atwood notes has concerning implications for code readability. However, the researchers concluded that for typical short online reading, font differences may not matter significantly. Atwood's key takeaway is that avoiding large text blocks is more important than font choice.
key_points: A 2001 usability study found 13% reading time difference between worst and best fonts, which researchers considered 'relatively small'
Courier, the only monospace font tested, performed second-worst—barely better than the completely inappropriate Corsiva
The connection between reading paragraphs in browsers and reading code in IDEs is unclear, but results don't favor monospace fonts
For short online passages with conventional fonts, reading speed differences may not have real consequences
Good design that avoids large text blocks matters more than font selection
money_quotes: Courier – the only monospace font in the experiment – was the second worst font for reading time.
Courier barely performed any better than the abhorrent Corsiva!
Any connection between reading paragraphs in a browser and reading code in an IDE is unclear, but it certainly doesn't bode well for the use of monospace fonts in general.
Before worrying about fonts, be sure you aren't presenting giant blocks of text to the user in the first place.
Well-designed web pages avoid using large blocks of text, and so should your code.
It's an amazing source of usability experiments with actual data, hypotheses, citations, statistics, and all that other stuff that puts the science back into computer science.
key_insight: While font choice matters less than we might think for short text, the poor performance of monospace fonts in reading studies should make developers question assumptions about code readability.
